# Figure description: Страт_колонки_скважин_черт_49_л_4.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/drill_holes_data/Страт_колонки_скважин_черт_49_л_4.jpg`  
**Generated by**: claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## Figure Type

This is a **composite stratigraphic/lithological borehole correlation panel** (сводный литолого-стратиграфический разрез / коррелятивная схема скважин) — a multi-column figure showing side-by-side borehole/well logs correlated across a survey area. It is a standard Soviet-era exploration document format used to demonstrate stratigraphic continuity and lithological variation between drill holes.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The figure covers multiple boreholes (скважины) drilled across the survey area in northern Kazakhstan, corresponding to map sheets **M-42** and/or **N-42** (1:200,000 scale). Based on the title blocks visible at the top and within the figure, this appears to be a correlation diagram associated with **Report 36572** (Smolianova et al., 1983–1984).

Several individual borehole logs are shown — at least **7–9 separate columns** are visible across the width of the figure. Individual column headers reference specific borehole numbers and localities, though most are partially illegible at this scan resolution. Some partially legible references include:

- **Скв. (скважина)** numbers — at least references to borehole designations such as **В-185, В-189** or similar (right-side panel header partially legible as "**Скважина В-185,198**" or similar)
- References to **Бандыкольской** (Bandykol') formation/area — visible in at least two column headers
- References to **Тенгизской** (Tengiz) area or formation — visible in the leftmost column header
- Depth ranges appear to extend from surface (0 m) down to approximately **300–500+ metres** based on column lengths

The logs are connected by **correlation lines** drawn horizontally and diagonally across the figure, linking equivalent stratigraphic horizons between boreholes.

---

## Key Content

### Column Structure
Each borehole column is drawn in the standard Soviet format with:
- A **lithological pattern column** (centre) showing rock types via standardized hatch/dot patterns
- **Depth markers** (in metres) on the left or right margin of each column
- **Stratigraphic index labels** (age/formation codes) on the outer margins
- **Sample or interval annotations** at specific horizons

### Lithological Patterns Observed
Multiple distinct lithological units are represented by standard Soviet geological survey symbols:
1. **Dotted/stippled pattern** — sandstone (песчаник) or coarse clastic material; this is the dominant pattern throughout most columns
2. **Brick/rectangle pattern** — limestone or carbonate rocks (известняк); visible in several intervals
3. **Horizontal line pattern** — shale, mudstone, or clay (глина/аргиллит)
4. **Cross-hatch or diagonal pattern** — possibly volcanic or intrusive material
5. **Solid black intervals** — possibly coal seams, dark shale, or mineralized zones; visible near the base of some columns

### Stratigraphic Labels
Stratigraphic age index labels (visible on column margins) include characters consistent with:
- **C** (Carboniferous — Каменноугольный) with subscripts (C₁, C₂, C₃)
- **D** (Devonian — Девонский) possibly at depth
- **P** (Permian — Пермский) in upper intervals
- **K** or **J** possibly (Cretaceous/Jurassic cover)
- Individual formation/suite codes partially legible as **"вк," "нм," "бк"** or similar Cyrillic abbreviations for local formation names

### Correlation Lines
Numerous **correlation tie-lines** connect equivalent horizons across boreholes. Several lines are nearly horizontal (indicating laterally continuous beds), while others dip significantly, indicating structural dip or facies changes between holes. Some lines diverge dramatically, suggesting **unconformities or faults** between certain borehole pairs.

### Fault/Unconformity Symbols
The **diagonal hatch symbol** (zigzag lines — "///") appears multiple times along the left edge of several columns, indicating **tectonic discontinuities, faults, or erosional unconformities** at those stratigraphic levels.

### Depth Scale
Depth increments appear to be marked every **10–20 metres** within each column. The total logged depth varies per borehole but appears to range from approximately **100 m to 500+ m**.
